title: Cards
description: Explore and implement various card styles, including small, large and image cards.
content:
This section introduces the different card styles available in the system, from compact small cards to visually rich image cards. Each example is paired with JSX code snippets, providing you with a practical guide to incorporate these components seamlessly into your project.

## Small Card

```jsx
<CardGrid>
  <Card
    title="Instructions"
    href="/docs/resource-home/installation"
    icon="alignJustify"
    variant="small"
    description="test description"
  />
  <Card
    title="Setup"
    href="/docs/resource-home/setup"
    icon="alignJustify"
    variant="small"
  />
  <Card
    title="Rubix Studios"
    href="https://rubixstudios.com.au"
    icon="alignJustify"
    external={true}
    variant="small"
  />
</CardGrid>
```

## Large Card

```jsx
<CardGrid>
  <Card
    subtitle="Instructions"
    title="Installation"
    description="Get started with Documents using our quick start installation guide to get your project started."
    href="/docs/resource-home/installation"
  />
  <Card
    subtitle="Setup"
    title="Site Settings"
    description="Setting up your Documents projects layout, links and search engine optimisation."
    href="/docs/resource-home/setup"
  />
  <Card
    subtitle="Support"
    title="Rubix Studios"
    description="Australia's leading branding, marketing and web development company."
    href="https://rubixstudios.com.au"
    external={true}
  />
</CardGrid>
```

## Image Card

```jsx
<CardGrid>
  <Card
    title="Instructions"
    href="/docs/introduction/installation"
    image="/images/og-image.png"
    variant="image"
  />
  <Card
    title="Setup"
    href="/docs/introduction/setup"
    image="/images/og-image.png"
    variant="image"
  />
  <Card
    title="Rubix Studios"
    href="https://www.rubixstudios.com.au"
    image="/images/og-image.png"
    external={true}
    variant="image"
  />
</CardGrid>
```

title: Diagrams
description: Add various diagram types, including flowcharts, decision trees and entity-relationship diagrams.
content:
Diagrams are powerful tools for visualizing processes, relationships, and decisions. This section showcases different types of diagrams created using **Mermaid**, complete with examples and reusable code snippets to integrate into your projects.

## Flowchart

A flowchart represents a sequence of steps or processes in a visual format. Use this diagram to map workflows, decision-making processes, or operational flows.

```jsx
<Mermaid chart="graph TD
Start --> Task1
Task1 --> Task2
Task2 --> End" />
```

## Decision Tree

Decision trees illustrate choices and possible outcomes, making them ideal for decision-making workflows or processes involving multiple paths.

```jsx
<Mermaid chart="graph TD
  A[Start] --> B{Is it raining?}
  B -->|Yes| C[Take an umbrella]
  B -->|No| D[Enjoy the weather]
  C --> E[Go outside]
  D --> E" 
/>
```

## Entity-Relationship Diagram

Entity-relationship diagrams (ERDs) are used to model relationships between entities in a system. They are widely used in database design and system architecture planning.

```jsx
<Mermaid chart="erDiagram
  CUSTOMER ||--o{ ORDER : places
  ORDER ||--|{ LINE-ITEM : contains
  PRODUCT ||--o{ LINE-ITEM : included in
  CUSTOMER {
    string name
    string email
  }
  ORDER {
    int orderNumber
    date orderDate
  }
  LINE-ITEM {
    int quantity
    float price
  }
  PRODUCT {
    int productId
    string name
    float price
  }" 
/>
```

Each of these diagrams serves a specific purpose and Mermaid makes it easy to generate them dynamically. Feel free to experiment with the provided code snippets and adapt them to your needs.

Entity-relationship diagrams (ERDs) are used to model relationships between entities in a system. They are widely used in database design and system architecture planning.

title: Filetree
description: This section provides an overview of file structures and their implementation using the FileTree component.
content:
This section demonstrates the structure of a file tree using the `FileTree` component. Below is an example showcasing folders and files organized hierarchically for a project setup.

## How to Use

Integrate the `FileTree` component into your project to visually represent file and folder structures. This is particularly useful for documentation, tutorials, or providing users with an overview of your project's architecture.

### JSX Code Example

Here is how you can define the file tree structure in for use in your project:

```jsx
<FileTree>
  <Folder name="src" label="Source Code">
    <File name="index.tsx" label="Index File" />
    <Folder name="components" label="Components">
      <File name="button.tsx" label="Button Component" />
      <File name="input.tsx" label="Input Component" />
    </Folder>
    <Folder name="pages" label="Pages">
      <File name="home.tsx" label="Home Page" />
      <File name="about.tsx" label="About Page" />
    </Folder>
  </Folder>
</FileTree>
```

Use this code as a template to set up your own file tree structure and customize it as needed.

title: Lists
description: This section provides an overview of creating and using lists in the Documents boilerplate.
content:
Learn how to create and render different types of lists in the Documents boilerplate. Examples include checklists, ordered lists, unordered lists and nested lists.

## Checklist

* \[x] Write the press release
* \[ ] Update the website
* \[ ] Contact the media

```jsx
- [x] Write the press release
- [ ] Update the website
- [ ] Contact the media
```

## Simple List

* Item 1
* Item 2
* Item 3

```jsx
- Item 1
- Item 2
- Item 3
```

## Number List

1. Research
2. Draft the content
3. Review and edit
4. Publish

```jsx
1. Research
2. Draft the content
3. Review and edit
4. Publish
```

## Nested List

* Main Category 1
  * Sub Item 1.1
  * Sub Item 1.2
* Main Category 2
  * Sub Item 2.1
  * Sub Item 2.2

```jsx
- Main Category 1
  - Sub Item 1.1
  - Sub Item 1.2
- Main Category 2
  - Sub Item 2.1
  - Sub Item 2.2
```

Use these examples as a foundation to create and customize lists that fit your project's needs.

title: Maths
description: This section provides an overview of Introduction.
content:

## Basic Algebra

The area of a circle ($$A$$) can be calculated using the radius ($$r$$) as follows:

```math
A = \pi r^2
```

## Quadratic Formula

The quadratic formula for solving an equation of the form $$ax^2 + bx + c = 0$$ is:

```math
x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}
```

## Newton's Second Law of Motion

Newton's second law of motion states that force ($$F$$) is the product of mass ($$m$$) and acceleration ($$a$$):

```math
F = ma
```

## Pythagorean Theorem

The Pythagorean theorem relates the lengths of the sides of a right triangle:

```math
a^2 + b^2 = c^2
```

## Einstein's Mass-Energy Equivalence

Einstein's famous equation relates energy ($$E$$), mass ($$m$$), and the speed of light ($$c$$):

```math
E = mc^2
```

title: Consortium
description: This page covers the procedures for consortium meetings.
content: ## Scheduling Consortium meetings should be held at least once a month, with the exact date decided by the Co-Presidents and Secretary as needed each month in order to accommodate the schedules of attendees. Upon scheduling consortium, the Secretary should send out a meeting reminder to all ASEC members and consortium attendees at least one (1) week before the scheduled meeting time. ## Guidelines Consortium meetings are intended as a space for clubs to collaborate and share information, in order to promote cooperation and mutual support among the participating school clubs, address any issues or concerns, and avoid scheduling conflicts between club events. The Co-Presidents and Secretary should ensure that the meetings are held in a safe and inclusive environment for all attendees, and that all attendees feel comfortable sharing their thoughts and opinions. ## Attendance & Venue Consortium meeting attendees include all ASEC members, the teacher supervisor of ASEC, as well as at least one (1) delegate from each school club (all clubs must send a delegate). The consortium should be held in a large room capable of fitting all attendees, with a projector, microphone, appropriate seating and optional podium. The seating should be arranged in a layout that resembles the image below (within the constraints of the room).

title: Internal Meetings
description: This page covers the procedures for internal ASEC meetings.
content:
## Scheduling

At the beginning of each term, the Co-Presidents should decide what day of the week that internal meetings should be held on, in order to avoid scheduling conflicts with other activities for the ASEC members.

**Note:** Internal ASEC meetings should run weekly. The Co-Presidents may change the day of the week that internal meetings are held on after the term starts in order to accommodate any changes, but they should not change the frequency of the meetings.

## Guidelines

Internal ASEC meetings are intended to be a space for the ASEC members to discuss any issues, events, policies and concerns, and to provide support to each other on their council-work. Internal meetings may also be used to provide updates on ASEC-invloved matters and review requests from the consortium.
These meetings are not intended to be held in a public forum, and are not to have spectators present (IE. no non-ASEC members should be present at internal meetings). The Co-Presidents and Secretary should ensure that the meetings are held in a safe and inclusive environment for all ASEC members, and that all members feel comfortable sharing their thoughts and opinions. Meeting reminders should be sent out via email to all ASEC members at least 24 hours before the scheduled meeting time, and the meeting agenda should be distributed at the start of the meeting.

## Attendance

The only people who may be present during an internal ASEC meeting are the Co-Presidents, the Secretary, Grade Representatives, Communications Lead, Student Wellbeing Lead, and any official advisors. The teacher supervisor of ASEC may also attend if they with to do so. No quorum is required for an internal meeting to take place, however a meeting may be disbanded by the member(s) in charge of the meeting if they feel that there are not enough members present to have a productive meeting. If a member is unable to attend an internal meeting, they should notify the Co-Presidents or Secretary as soon as possible, and provide any relevant updates or information that they would like to be discussed during the meeting.

## Getting Started

title: Resource Home
description: This is the homepage for the ASEC resource portal. You can find most important links and resources here.
content:
![Banner](/images/banner.png "Documents")

## Quicklinks

Below are links to some important documents you can find on this site.

## Site Info

This site is internal to ASEC and is not intended to be accessed by external parties. It is intended to be a resource for ASEC members to find important information, documents, and resources related to their council work. Do not share your access code with anyone. If you believe your access code has been compromized, please contact the Secretary immediately to have it regenerated.

title: Members
description: This is a list of all the current members of ASEC.
content:
Members are split into 3 catagories in order of seniority: Senior Leadership, Associate (Assoc.) Leadership, and Official Advisors.

## Senior Leadership

The highest ranked managing members of ASEC (based on administrative responsibilities and seniority). These members are responsible for tasks including high level decision making, overseeing the general operations of ASEC, providing support to the rest of the council, and/or representing a constituency.

## Assoc. Leadership

The intermediate ranked managing members of ASEC (based on administrative responsibilities and seniority). These members are responsible for managing their own specialized teams and providing support to the rest of the council.

***

## Official Advisors

The Official Advisors are a non-managing category of members within ASEC. They are responsible for providing support and advice to their respective council members, but don't make administrative decisions. They may perform certain tasks as delegated by their respective council members and approved by a Co-President.
